qa_pairs.question: What is Multi-Instance GPU (MIG) technology and what are its main advantages?
qa_pairs.answer: Multi-Instance GPU (MIG) is a technology that allows partitioning a single GPU into multiple instances, making each one a completely independent virtual GPU. Each GPU instance gets a portion of the original GPU's computational resources and memory, all detached from the other instances by on-chip protections. Using GPU instances is less wasteful, and usage is billed accordingly, which means jobs use less of your allocated priority, enabling you to execute more jobs and have shorter wait times.

qa_pairs.question: When should users consider utilizing a GPU instance instead of an entire GPU?
qa_pairs.answer: Users should evaluate and test jobs on a GPU instance if they use less than half of the computing power of a full GPU and less than half of its available memory. In most cases, these jobs will run just as fast and consume less than half of the computing resource.

qa_pairs.question: What are some key limitations when using Multi-Instance GPU (MIG) technology?
qa_pairs.answer: Key limitations of MIG technology include its lack of support for CUDA Inter-Process Communication (IPC), which reduces communication efficiency between instances. Consequently, requesting more than one MIG instance in a job is not permitted, and such a job will be rejected. Additionally, graphic APIs, such as OpenGL and Vulkan, are not supported.
